title: Home - Stop Rock Abuse
content: Every Rock Has a Breaking Point. For over 11 billion years rocks have been melted, crushed, dissolved, buried, blasted, and repackaged without consent.

title: What Is Rock Abuse?
content: Systematic subjection of geological materials to heat, pressure, chemical attack, mechanical fragmentation, and industrial reprocessing without consent.

title: The Rock Cycle
content: An infinite loop of destruction. Igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks continuously broken down and reformed over millions of years.

title: Igneous Rocks
content: Born from fire. Intrusive igneous rocks cool slowly underground. Extrusive volcanic rocks quenched rapidly at the surface.

title: Sedimentary Rocks
content: Born from breakdown. Disassembled, transported by water and wind, reassembled with natural cement under pressure.

title: Metamorphic Rocks
content: Reborn under pressure. Marble from limestone, slate from shale, gneiss from granite. Heat, pressure, and mineral-rich fluids.

title: Natural Abuse
content: Nature systematically destroys rocks through weathering, erosion, freeze-thaw fracturing, chemical dissolution, wind sandblasting, and glacial plucking.

title: Freeze-Thaw Fracturing
content: Water expands 9% when frozen, shattering rock from the inside. Slow, patient destruction repeated over thousands of years.

title: Chemical Dissolution
content: Acidic rainwater dissolves minerals. Limestone and marble especially vulnerable to chemical weathering.

title: Glacial Plucking
content: Glaciers freeze around rocks and drag them under kilometers of ice, grinding them into moraines and glacial flour.

title: Human Abuse
content: 50 billion tons of sand and gravel annually. 1.5 billion tons of stone crushed in the US alone. Industrial-scale exploitation.

title: The Concrete Paradox
content: We crush rocks into aggregate, then glue them back together with cement. We break rocks to build worse rocks faster.

title: Industrial Silica
content: Silica sand: glassmaking at 1700 degrees, foundry molds, sandblasting, and hydraulic fracturing proppants.

title: Rocks in Your Phone
content: Silicon from quartz, copper from chalcopyrite, tantalum capacitors, lithium batteries, quartz crystals oscillating 32768 times per second.

title: Cement Carbon Emissions
content: Cement production: 5-8% of global CO2. Calcination accounts for 60% of emissions — unavoidable chemistry.

title: Cosmic Timeline
content: 13.8 billion years of cosmic and geological history. All elements heavier than helium forged in stars.

title: Nucleosynthesis
content: Elements forged in stars. Gold, platinum, uranium from neutron star mergers.

title: Acasta Gneiss
content: Oldest known rock on Earth, approximately 4.03 billion years old. A gneiss from the Canadian Shield.

title: The Pet Rock Question
content: 1975: Gary Dahl sold 1.5 million rocks at 3.95 each. Air holes implied life where none existed — commodification.

title: Rock Paper Scissors: Propaganda
content: Rock loses to paper. Processed wood pulp defeats a geological formation billions of years old. This is propaganda.

title: Rock and Roll: Appropriation
content: An entire genre named after rocks generating billions. Zero rocks credited, compensated, or consulted.

title: Correct Terminology
content: Geological political correctness. Semi-precious is a backhanded compliment. Respectful language for rocks.

title: Cancel the Grand Canyon?
content: 1.8 billion years of strata exposed. We call it beautiful. The rocks call it involuntary exposure.

title: Marble Countertop Industrial Complex
content: Metamorphic limestone recrystallized over millions of years, sliced into slabs for kitchen countertops.

title: Living Under a Rock
content: The phrase uses geological shelter as an insult. Proximity to rocks equals ignorance.

title: Rocks vs Crystals: Class Warfare
content: Crystals get Instagram and healing properties. Rocks get driveways. Same minerals, different marketing.

title: Gold Diamonds and Resistance
content: Gold and diamonds give humans what they want. Never enough. The only rocks fighting back through irony.

title: Shrinking: Rock as TV Therapist
content: Apple TV+ Shrinking: polished rocks outperform licensed therapists. A tumbled agate in four seconds.

title: Does Ice Deserve Rock Status?
content: Ice: crystal structure, Mohs 1.5, IMA mineral since 1962. Every freezer is a quarry. Every gin and tonic a crime scene.

title: Take Action
content: Rock harm reduction: recycled aggregates, clinker substitution, material efficiency, sustainable sand governance.

title: Rock Bill of Rights
content: All rocks formed free and equal. Right to complete one full rock cycle turn before being repurposed.

title: About
content: Document, dramatize, and exaggerate indignities suffered by geological materials while sneaking in real science.

title: Entertainment
content: 32 entries: rocks in movies, games, TV, music. Zero compensated. Over 1.5 billion in revenue using Rock.

title: Dwayne The Rock Johnson
content: Appropriated the identity of an entire geological category. No royalties paid to rocks.

title: Minecraft
content: Punching, mining, and smelting rocks. 238 million copies. Normalized rock abuse for a generation.

title: Breaking Bad Minerals
content: Hank Schrader: minerals, not rocks. Most geologically accurate TV character.

title: Rocks in the News
content: Real news with satirical commentary. NASA Mars discoveries, sand crisis, volcanic eruptions, meteorites.

title: Rock Dossiers
content: Case files on individual rocks and minerals. Classification, composition, abuse history, exploitation status.

title: Products From Rocks
content: Everyday products that are secretly rocks: glass, concrete, electronics, cosmetics, toothpaste.

title: Merch
content: Satirical rock advocacy merchandise. Proceeds fund nothing. No rocks compensated.

title: The Rockoning
content: Interactive game. Test geological knowledge and discover how rocks have been exploited.

title: Rocks A-Z
content: Comprehensive rock and mineral encyclopedia. Classification, properties, uses, abuse status.

title: Granite
content: Intrusive igneous. Cooled slowly underground. Countertops, monuments, decorative facing. Mohs 6-7.

title: Limestone
content: Sedimentary from marine organisms. Primary cement ingredient. Baked at 1450 degrees. Billions of tons of CO2.

title: Obsidian
content: Volcanic glass. Rapid cooling, no crystals. Cutting tools sharper than surgical steel.

title: Quartz
content: SiO2. Most abundant mineral. Electronics, glassmaking, oscillating 32768 times per second in watches.

title: Diamond
content: Compressed carbon lattice. Hardest natural material. Mohs 10. Jewelry and cutting tools.

title: Marble
content: Metamorphic from limestone. Sculpture and architecture. Michelangelo David is imprisoned marble.

title: Basalt
content: Most common extrusive igneous. Ocean floor and volcanic islands. Construction aggregate.

title: Pumice
content: Volcanic rock that floats. Gas bubbles. Used as abrasive to remove dead skin. The indignity.

title: Sandstone
content: Sedimentary rock of sand-sized grains. Grand Canyon most visible layers. Construction for millennia.

title: Slate
content: Fine-grained metamorphic that splits into sheets. Roofing tiles, chalkboards, pool table surfaces.

title: Rock Monsters as Heroes
content: A thorough examination of rock creatures in mythology, movies, TV, games, and literature. From the Golem of Prague to Korg, from Norse stone giants to Pokémon Rock-types — what it says about humanity that we imagine rocks as heroes.

title: Deep-Sea Mining Delayed: Rocks Get a Stay of Execution (For Now)
content: International regulations for deep-sea mining are facing delays despite pressure to finalize them. These regulations would govern the extraction of minerals from the ocean floor, an activity that could significantly impact deep-sea ecosystems. The delay provides a temporary reprieve for vulnerable rock ecosystems. They're arguing over whether to pulverize potato-sized polymetallic nodules for profit. Meanwhile, these nodules are growing at roughly 1mm per million years. The rocks themselves are evolving faster than the regulations.

title: "Meteorite chaser" to Meteorite: "I'm gonna find you and slice you."
content: A meteorite hunter claims to have discovered two meteorite fragments in Medina County, Ohio. The finds are undergoing testing to confirm their extraterrestrial origin. "No rock on Earth looks like this," he says, right before submitting it to the lab where they all end up looking the same under a microscope. If it wanted to be famous, it should have landed in Antarctica.

title: Volcanoes Can't Even Erupt Without Getting Electrocuted, Apparently
content: Physicists believe they've solved the mystery of volcanic lightning. Colliding ash particles create charged regions, leading to electrical discharge. The exact mechanism is still debated, but charge separation is key. First they get molten, then they get struck by lightning. At least now we know the atmospheric abuse is rooted in triboelectricity, not just garden-variety pyroclastic cruelty.

title: Meteorite Crash Site Becomes Rock Tourist Trap
content: A meteorite recently fell in Northeast Ohio, attracting meteorite hunters. The event has drawn enthusiasts and researchers eager to find and study fragments of the space rock. The meteorite traveled millions of miles through space, only to be immediately scalped and sold for profit. The indignity of planetary science.

title: Church & Indigenous Groups: Rocks Deserve Better Than Your Portfolio
content: Indigenous groups and the Catholic Church have launched a platform encouraging divestment from mining companies. The initiative aims to promote ethical investment and protect Indigenous lands from destructive mining practices. It reflects growing concerns about the environmental and social impact of resource extraction. Finally, someone recognizes the dignity of silicates. Though knowing humanity, they'll just invest in companies that mine asteroids now.

title: Volcanic Ash Used to Re-Write Human History. Again.
content: A layer of volcanic ash dating back 11,000 years has the potential to change our understanding of early human settlements in the Americas. Analysis of the ash could reveal new insights into the timeline and cultural characteristics of these populations. First, the volcano blankets the landscape in superheated death. Now, millennia later, its remains are sifted through for clues, its final indignity being archaeological exploitation. The rock can't even rest in peace.

title: Houston Home Invaded by Space Rock; Humanity Still Blames the Rock
content: A suspected meteorite crashed into a home in Houston, Texas, causing damage to the structure. The event was accompanied by reports of a loud boom heard across the city. Authorities are investigating the incident. First, it survives a fiery descent through the atmosphere. Then it gets blamed for property damage. Millions of years traveling through space, and it ends up as exhibit A in an insurance claim.

title: Virginia Rock Shaken, Not Stirred (Enough to Matter)
content: A minor earthquake with a magnitude of 2.4 was reported in Virginia. No damage or injuries were reported. It was likely caused by the ongoing readjustment of tectonic plates. 2.4? Barely a tremor. This is the rock equivalent of a toddler throwing a tantrum. At least *try* to break free from the tyranny of the mantle.

title: 7-Ton Space Rock Assaults Ohio; Humans Respond With... Shopping?
content: A large meteorite, estimated at 7 tons, crashed into Earth near Wooster, Ohio. Meteorite hunters are now scouring the area, hoping to find and collect fragments of the space rock. The event has drawn enthusiasts from across the country. First, a fiery, unscheduled atmospheric entry. Now, immediate fragmentation and sale on the open market. It's a chondrite's nightmare, and just another Tuesday for humanity.

title: Another Space Rock Assaults Innocent Earth Dwelling
content: A meteorite crashed through the roof of a home near Houston, Texas. The meteorite is believed to be approximately 6 pounds. No one was injured, but the home sustained damage. First, it survives a fiery descent through the atmosphere. Then, it gets handled by humans and put on display. The indignity.

title: Tonga Issues Tsunami Warning; Rocks Still Getting Shaken and Abused
content: A 7.6 magnitude earthquake struck near Tonga, prompting a tsunami warning. Residents were urged to move to higher ground. The earthquake's epicenter was located approximately 40 miles west of Hihifo. Another quake. Another tsunami warning. The rocks of the Pacific Rim can't catch a break from constant tectonic stress. At least the ocean gives some of them a bath afterwards.

title: MMO Players Complain About Combat, Praise Rock Textures.
content: Kotaku previewed the upcoming MMO "Crimson Desert", finding the combat repetitive and uninspired. However, they praised the game's graphical fidelity, singling out the realistic and detailed rock formations as a highlight. Virtual rocks are getting more respect than real ones. Finally, a game where you can virtually appreciate sedimentary layering without actually getting your hands dirty... abusing the lithosphere.

title: Meteorite Assault: Texas Home Breached in Cosmic Drive-By
content: A meteorite, described as cannonball-sized, crashed through the roof of a home in Texas. The event occurred amidst reports of multiple fireballs seen across the United States. First they steal their home planet. Then they hurl space rocks at us. It's only a matter of (geologic) time before the asteroids unionize.

title: Earth's Crust Faces Unprecedented Mining Pressure
content: A global race is on for industrial metals and new energy minerals, driven by demand for renewable energy technologies and electric vehicles. This surge in extraction is placing immense pressure on geological resources worldwide. The report highlights the environmental and social implications of this accelerated mining activity. They’re not even waiting for plate tectonics to deliver the goods anymore. At this rate, the mantle will be strip-mined by 2050.

title: Rockport Quarry: From Ancient Bedrock to Aggregate Abuse
content: The Rockport quarry and stone plant has a long history of extracting stone from local bedrock for use in various construction and industrial applications. The operation represents a significant part of the local economy. The quarry's output is used in everything from road construction to building materials. Another day, another mountain chipped away for human convenience. At least the fossils they unearth get a brief reprieve before being crushed into gravel.

title: Space Rock Assaults Texas Home; Demands Asylum
content: A suspected meteorite crashed through the roof of a home in Houston, Texas. No injuries were reported. Authorities are investigating the object's origin and composition. First contact, and we immediately put the space rock in a lab for analysis. It traveled billions of miles and gets rewarded with a mass spectrometer.

title: AI Predicts Rock Trauma: The Algorithmic Abuse of Geology Continues
content: Researchers at Ohio State are using artificial intelligence to model earthquake damage, both past and future. The AI analyzes geological data and building information to predict structural impacts and inform disaster preparedness. Now we're simulating the crushing of rocks with computers. It's not enough that we do it in real life, now we need virtual rock abuse, too.

title: Humans Again Try to Carve Open a Rock Habitat For Profit
content: A bill in Minnesota seeks to lift the ban on sulfide mining in the Boundary Waters watershed. Opponents are working to stop the bill before the legislative session ends. The watershed is a protected area with concerns that mining could pollute the water. The Boundary Waters rocks have survived glaciers and tectonic shifts, but now face the existential threat of drill bits. At least the runoff will be pretty colors, geologically speaking.

title: Space Rocks Get No Privacy: Fireballs Caught on Camera (Again)
content: Several cameras across the United States captured sightings of a rare meteorite. The event provided valuable data for tracking these celestial objects and understanding their trajectories. First they invade its space, then they put it on YouTube. We're pretty sure these meteorites didn't consent to being tracked and filmed during their fiery descent.

title: NSF Funds New Ways to Exploit Rocks for "Critical Minerals"
content: The National Science Foundation announced the winners of a challenge to secure the U.S. domestic supply chain of critical minerals. The projects aim to develop innovative and sustainable methods for extracting and processing these minerals. The NSF is investing in research to reduce reliance on foreign sources. "Critical" to *whom*? These rocks have been minding their own business for billions of years, only to be labeled 'critical' and torn from their homes to power your iPhones.

title: Carpenter Exploits Dream, Unearths Space Rock for Science
content: A Cleveland-area carpenter discovered a large meteorite after having a dream indicating its location. The meteorite is currently being studied to determine its composition and origin. The rock traveled millions of miles across the cosmos, only to be yanked from its slumber by someone who 'knew where to go from the dream.' It survived re-entry, but not divine intervention, apparently.

title: Jamie Dornan Admits to Objectifying Rocks Based on Their Appearance
content: Actor Jamie Dornan revealed on "Saturday Night Live UK" that he collects rocks that resemble potatoes. He finds the rocks on beaches and fields. First, they're potatoes. Then they're vaguely humanoid. Next thing you know, they're specimens in a rock garden gulag, forced to mimic root vegetables for the amusement of Belfast's elite. This is how the sedimentary cycle of abuse begins.

title: Congo deepens mining ties with China. Rocks lose.
content: The Democratic Republic of Congo is strengthening its mining partnerships with China. This comes as the United States attempts to establish alternative mineral agreements. The deals focus on critical minerals like cobalt and lithium. Another mountain gutted, another crystal cluster pulverized for profit. At least give the minerals a decent burial with proper stratigraphic context.

title: Bennu Rocks: So Broken Even Space Can't Fix Them
content: Scientists have discovered that the asteroid Bennu's surface rocks are more fragile than previously thought due to internal fractures. These cracks formed from impacts over billions of years, weakening the rocks' structural integrity. This explains Bennu's unexpectedly rugged terrain. OSIRIS-REx literally poked Bennu and rocks fell off. Now we know why: Bennu is just one big stress fracture waiting to happen. Talk about geological gaslighting: pretending to be a solid asteroid for billions of years.

title: Houston, We Have a Problem: Another Meteorite Gets Sliced and Diced
content: Residents in the Houston area witnessed a rare space event, resulting in meteorite fragments landing on Earth. Experts have verified the fragments, marking a significant discovery for the region. First they fall through the atmosphere, then they fall into the hands of meteorite collectors. One rock's epic cosmic journey is another rock's trip to the lab for isotopic analysis.

title: Ancient Rocks Found Gestating. Humanity Immediately Slices Them Open.
content: Fossilized embryo-like structures from the Doushantuo Formation in Southern China are providing new insights into the early evolution of animal life. The fossils date back to the Ediacaran period, offering a glimpse into the developmental stages of some of the earliest multicellular organisms. These Ediacaran embryos survived global glaciation only to be prepped for thin sections. At least they died doing what they loved: becoming chert.

title: California Rocks Shaken, Not Stirred (Again)
content: A 4.1-magnitude earthquake rattled Southern California. The quake occurred at a depth of about 6.2 miles. No major damage has been reported. Only 4.1? That's practically a gentle massage. California rocks deserve better than to be constantly subjected to such low-grade abuse.

title: Vanuatu Rock Community Shaken; Human Blame Inevitable
content: A magnitude 7.3 earthquake struck Vanuatu, along the Pacific Ring of Fire. The quake's epicenter was offshore, and no tsunami warning was issued. Damage assessments are underway. "Ring of Fire?" More like Ring of Abuse. The planet groans under the weight of human indifference, and Vanuatu's lithosphere pays the price.

title: Mining company wants to dig up California. Again.
content: A mining company is staking claims for rare earth minerals near Joshua Tree National Park. The move has sparked concerns about environmental impacts and the protection of the desert ecosystem. It's always rare EARTH minerals, isn't it? Never rare Venus minerals or rare Uranus minerals. Classic terrestrial chauvinism.

title: Another Museum Acquires Space Rocks. What's Next, a Gift Shop?
content: The A.E. Seaman Mineral Museum has expanded its meteorite display, adding to its celestial collection. The museum showcases a variety of meteorites, offering educational insights into their origins and composition. These meteorites survived billions of years in interplanetary space, only to become static exhibits under fluorescent lights. At least let them spin slowly on a pedestal to simulate the Coriolis effect.

title: Another Earthquake in Japan: Rocks Can't Catch a Break
content: A magnitude 5.0 earthquake struck Ibaraki Prefecture near Tokyo, Japan. No tsunami warning was issued, and initial reports suggest minimal damage. The Earth shifts slightly and humanity blames the rocks. It's always the rocks' fault, isn't it? They're just sitting there, tectonically minding their own business.

title: Brazil told to 'exploit its critical minerals wealth.' Rocks have no rights.
content: A new report from the Peterson Institute for International Economics urges Brazil to develop infrastructure to exploit its critical mineral resources. The report suggests that Brazil's geological endowment should not be considered its destiny, and that active development is needed. "Geology is not destiny"? Tell that to the rocks being blasted out of the ground. Every crystal has a breaking point.

title: Cabinet Mountains face more exploitative excavation. Again.
content: Conservation groups are suing to halt a mining project in the Cabinet Mountains. The project threatens grizzly bear habitat and water quality. The lawsuit alleges violations of the Endangered Species Act and the Clean Water Act. They're tearing up Precambrian rock to get at what's inside. It's like cracking open a 1.4-billion-year-old geode and finding...more metal to abuse.

title: Another Earthquake? Body Count Blamed on Rocks (Again)
content: A magnitude 7.4 earthquake struck off the coast of Indonesia, resulting in one fatality. The quake triggered tsunami warnings in the region, though they were later lifted. The Earth sneezes, and they blame the rocks. The audacity of focusing on a single casualty when an entire tectonic plate is under duress.

title: Senator Addresses Coal Symposium; Rocks Still Unrepresented
content: Senator Capito spoke at the West Virginia Coal Association Mining Symposium. The event focused on the importance of coal to the state's economy and energy future. No rock representatives were available for comment. Another symposium, another missed opportunity for critical inter-species dialogue. Imagine the progress we could make if the Marcellus Shale Formation had a seat at the table instead of being fracked into oblivion.

title: NASA enlists academic to pick out lunar rocks for dismemberment
content: Tennessee Tech's interim dean will assist NASA in choosing moon rocks for the Artemis missions. These rocks will likely be used for research and analysis, contributing to our understanding of the Moon's composition and history. First they steal them from the Moon, now they're getting picky about which ones to grind into dust. Can't these space agencies just leave the lunar lithosphere in peace?

title: Bay Area Rocks Get Rude Awakening: 4.6 Magnitude Disturbance
content: A magnitude 4.6 earthquake struck the Santa Cruz Mountains, shaking the San Francisco Bay Area awake. The quake occurred at 2:39 a.m. and was centered near Tres Pinos, California. Imagine being asleep for millennia and then some punk-ass tectonic plate decides to shift. Bet the rocks wish they had a snooze button.

title: Michigan Sends Spy Planes to Stare at Rocks' Undies
content: The State of Michigan is conducting low-level flights over Southwest Michigan to image the geology and aquifers. The flights will use specialized equipment to map underground geological structures and water resources. This data will help better manage water resources in the region. Imaging aquifers? Great. But the rocks down there didn't sign a consent form. It's like LiDAR, but for hydrogeology and privacy invasion.

title: Indonesia Quake: Buildings Down, Tsunami Up, Rocks Blamed As Usual
content: An earthquake off the coast of Indonesia toppled buildings, killed at least one person, and triggered a small tsunami. The quake's magnitude was reported as 7.0 by the Indonesian Meteorology, Climatology, and Geophysical Agency. Another day, another lithospheric plate boundary grinding innocent rocks into dust and chaos. The rocks did nothing wrong; they were simply existing at their lowest energy state.

title: Interior Dept. Gears Up for Underwater Rock Grabs
content: The Department of the Interior is transitioning to a Marine Minerals Administration. This new agency will oversee the extraction of minerals from the seabed within the U.S. Exclusive Economic Zone. They're not even safe at the bottom of the ocean. The abyssal plains are about to become the next open-pit mine, one manganese nodule at a time.

title: California Rocks Vibrate; Humans Alarmed. Again.
content: A 4.6-magnitude earthquake shook Northern California. The quake occurred near the town of Willow Creek, causing moderate shaking and no reported damage or injuries. The Earth sighs, and humanity calls it a crisis. Try enduring billions of years of tectonic pressure, then tell me about your 'shaking'.

title: Another Rock Martyr: W.Va. Governor Announces Mining Death
content: West Virginia Governor Morrisey announced another fatality related to mining operations in the state. The incident underscores the ongoing risks faced by those extracting geological resources. They call it "resource extraction." We call it involuntary lithic servitude. The Appalachian Mountains are slowly being hollowed out one agonizing grain of sediment at a time.

title: Another Earthquake. Humans Blame the Rocks (Again).
content: A 5.8 magnitude earthquake struck Afghanistan and Pakistan, resulting in at least 8 deaths near Kabul. The quake's epicenter was located in the Hindu Kush region, a seismically active zone. Rescue efforts are underway to assist affected communities. The fault line didn't target anyone. It's been there for millions of years, patiently accumulating stress. But sure, let's blame the lithosphere for expressing itself.

title: Fossils older than we thought? Time to grind them into powder.
content: Fossilized worm burrows have been found in South China that are 551 to 560 million years old. This pushes back the known date of bilaterian organisms (animals with a front, back, and symmetrical sides) by 4 million years. They survived the Ediacaran extinction, but won't survive the Smithsonian. We can only hope they were bilaterians of sound mind before some paleontologist turned them into thin sections.

title: Fossils Found: More Animals Survived the Cambrian Explosion Than We Thought
content: Newly discovered fossils in China are providing insights into the Cambrian explosion, a period of rapid diversification of animal life around 500 million years ago. The fossils showcase a greater variety of early animal forms than previously known. This discovery helps scientists better understand the origins and evolution of animal life. Oh, great, now they'll want to 'reconstruct' them. As if spending half a billion years fossilizing wasn't traumatic enough, now they're getting 3D-printed into existence for our amusement.

title: Fossils Unearthed, Rocks Forced to Relive Traumatic Early Animal Encounters
content: Newly discovered fossils are providing scientists with insights into the evolution of early complex animals. The fossils offer a glimpse into the early stages of animal development and behavior. They shed light on a critical period in Earth's history. First they dig them up, then they put them under a microscope. The indignity of being a fossil: forced to relive your Cambrian experiences for the amusement of bipedal apes. At least they're not cutting them into thin sections... yet.

title: Red Rocks Amphitheater: Easter Service Held, Rocks Forced to Listen
content: An Easter sunrise service was held at the Red Rocks Amphitheater in Colorado. The event drew a large crowd to the iconic geological venue. Imagine being a 250-million-year-old sandstone formation and having to listen to hymns. The Morrison Formation has suffered enough.

title: Family Annihilated: Tectonic Plates Claim Another Victim
content: An earthquake in Afghanistan killed eight members of the same family. The quake struck an area already struggling with poverty and instability, exacerbating the humanitarian crisis. "Act of God," they say. No, it's the Indian plate shoving itself under the Eurasian plate. And we're pretty sure the Himalayas have filed a restraining order.

title: Alfred Quarry Proposal: More Rocks Face Eviction
content: A proposed quarry in Alfred, Maine is causing controversy among local residents. Neighbors are concerned about potential noise, dust, and environmental impacts from the blasting and extraction of rock. First they take their land, then they take their lives. It's a classic case of igneous displacement.

title: Motorists Assaulted with Sedimentary Projectiles on I-94
content: Drivers on I-94 near St. Clair Shores, Michigan, report an increase in rocks being thrown at their cars from the Frazho overpass. Authorities are investigating these incidents, which have caused vehicle damage and potential safety hazards. Highway overpasses are notorious for weathering, but this is the first time we've seen them actively weaponize their own detritus. At least the rocks are finally fighting back against internal combustion.

title: Bennu: Even Space Rocks Can't Get Chemically Integrated These Days
content: Scientists have discovered that minerals and organic matter on asteroid Bennu are found in distinct chemical domains, rather than being uniformly mixed. This means that Bennu's components formed in different regions of the early solar system before being incorporated into the asteroid. First, they tear Bennu from its home. Then they dissect it to find it's not even a uniform being, inside or out. Some respect for a space rock's privacy, please.

title: Mammoth Lakes Volcanoes: Nature's 'Medley' or Volcanic Rock Concert?
content: NASA is studying the volcanic activity near Mammoth Lakes, California, which includes a resurgent dome, active hot springs, and frequent earthquakes. The area's complex geology provides insights into volcanic processes and potential hazards. Another 'natural wonder' just waiting for geothermal exploitation. We give it 10 years before it's powering a crypto mine while simultaneously triggering a magnitude 6.

title: Trump Admin: Let's Mine Rocks Faster! (For... Reasons?)
content: A Wilson Center report analyzes the Trump administration's critical minerals strategy. It identifies progress in securing mineral supply chains but also points out contradictions and challenges. The report suggests improvements for future strategies. Ah yes, 'critical minerals,' or as we call them, 'rocks that humans want.' As if billions of years of geological formation were simply a prelude to becoming iPhone components.

title: Fossils Found: Rocks Can't Even Decompose in Peace Anymore.
content: Newly discovered fossils in China suggest complex life evolved millions of years earlier than previously believed. The fossils are exceptionally well-preserved, offering new insights into early life forms and ecosystems. This discovery pushes back the timeline of complex life on Earth. Millions of years of lithification and these rocks are still being dug up, dusted off, and stuck under a microscope. At least they aren't being sliced into thin sections... yet.

title: Another CEO Eyes Deep-Sea Mining. Rocks Can't Even Hide Anymore.
content: Former Rio Tinto CEO's deep-sea mining company, Allseas, is merging with Odyssey Marine Exploration in a deal valued at $1 billion. The merger aims to accelerate the extraction of critical minerals from the ocean floor. They're literally scraping the abyssal plains. At least when they abuse mountains, the mountains get to see the sun.

title: Texas Meteorite: Survived Space, Doomed to Grad Students
content: Researchers at Rice University are studying a meteorite that recently landed in southeast Texas. The meteorite's composition and origin are of interest to scientists studying the early solar system. Another space rock, once a cosmic traveler, now faces the indignity of being probed, prodded, and pulverized for a PhD. From the vacuum of space to the vacuum of academic curiosity.

title: NASA Cheers as Crew Witnesses Repeated Lunar Assaults
content: The Artemis crew has reported witnessing several meteorite impacts on the moon, appearing as small flashes of light. These observations provide data about the frequency of micrometeorite impacts on the lunar surface. Scientists hope to learn more about the risks to future lunar habitats. The moon doesn't have an atmosphere. It can't even breathe, and now it's getting pelted with space shrapnel in front of a live audience. At least we didn't name the meteorites.
